---
title: "Giorgio Maarraoui"
---

::: {.hero-panel}
::: {.hero-grid}
::: {.hero-copy}
![](img/portrait.jpg){width=190 .portrait}

## PhD Candidate in Economics  
University of Manchester

I work on **climate change, development, public economics, and applied microeconometrics**, with a focus on how climate shocks shape adaptation, conflict, migration, and welfare in Sub-Saharan Africa. My research combines **household micro-data**, **high-resolution spatial data**, and **structural spatial equilibrium modelling** to study both mechanisms and policy counterfactuals.

Alongside my academic work, I contribute to applied distributional analysis at the **World Bank Poverty & Equity Global Practice**, where I work on **Commitment to Equity (CEQ)** assessments and related fiscal-incidence research.
:::

::: {.hero-aside}
### Current Roles

**University of Manchester**  
PhD Candidate in Economics

**World Bank**  
Economic Consultant, Poverty & Equity Global Practice

### Research Areas

- Climate adaptation and welfare
- Fiscal incidence and inequality
- Migration, conflict, and spatial development
- Applied econometrics and policy evaluation

### Links

- [CV](cv/Giorgio_Maarraoui_CV.pdf)
- [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=YmJMv2wAAAAJ&hl=en)
- [ORCID](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-2749-5782)
- [Teaching](teaching.qmd)
:::
:::
:::

::: {.section-grid}
::: {.feature-card}
### Research Agenda

My dissertation develops a unified research agenda on the economics of climate stress. The three core papers examine: how Nigerian farm households adapt production decisions to heat shocks; how rainfall shocks alter conflict transitions in transhumant Africa; and how climate shocks, migration frictions, trade, and conflict interact in spatial equilibrium. Together, the papers connect micro-level adaptation to regional welfare outcomes.
:::

::: {.feature-card}
### Ongoing Applied Work

At the World Bank, I work on fiscal-incidence and equity analysis using the CEQ framework. Current projects include **forthcoming 2025 CEQ studies for Pakistan and Djibouti**, alongside earlier work on poverty, inequality, and gender in Djibouti. This policy work complements my academic research by extending distributional analysis into directly policy-relevant settings.
:::
:::

::: {.feature-card}
### Teaching and Course Design

I teach across microeconomics and econometrics at the University of Manchester, including **Intermediate Microeconomics**, **Econometrics**, and **Applied Microeconometrics (MSc)**. I have also **lectured and redesigned the MSc Microeconometrics course**, strengthening its empirical focus, structure, and delivery.
:::
